title: Prelude to Songs Before Sunrise
content:
В В В В Between the green bud and the red
В В В В Youth sat and sang by Time, and shed
В В В В From eyes and tresses flowers and tears,
В В В В From heart and spirit hopes and fears,
В В В В Upon the hollow stream whose bed
В В В В Is channelled by the foamless years;
В В В В And with the white the gold-haired head
В В В В Mixed running locks, and in TimeпїЅs ears
В В В В YouthпїЅs dreams hung singing, and TimeпїЅs truth
В В В В Was half not harsh in the ears of Youth.

В В В В Between the bud and the blown flower
В В В В Youth talked with joy and grief an hour,
В В В В With footless joy and wingless grief
В В В В And twin-born faith and disbelief
В В В В Who share the seasons to devour;
В В В В And long ere these made up their sheaf
В В В В Felt the winds round him shake and shower
В В В В The rose-red and the blood-red leaf,
В В В В Delight whose germ grew never grain,
В В В В And passion dyed in its own pain.

В В В В Then he stood up, and trod to dust
В В В В Fear and desire, mistrust and trust,
В В В В And dreams of bitter sleep and sweet,
В В В В And bound for sandals on his feet
В В В В Knowledge and patience of what must
В В В В And what things may be, in the heat
В В В В And cold of years that rot and rust
В В В В And alter; and his spiritпїЅs meat
В В В В Was freedom, and his staff was wrought
В В В В Of strength, and his cloak woven of thought.

В В В В For what has he whose will sees clear
В В В В To do with doubt and faith and fear,
В В В В Swift hopes and slow despondencies?
В В В В His heart is equal with the seaпїЅs
В В В В And with the sea-windпїЅs, and his ear
В В В В Is level to the speech of these,
В В В В And his soul communes and takes cheer
В В В В With the actual earthпїЅs equalities,
В В В В Air, light, and night, hills, winds, and streams,
В В В В And seeks not strength from strengthless dreams.

В В В В His soul is even with the sun
В В В В Whose spirit and whose eye are one,
В В В В Who seeks not stars by day, nor light
В В В В And heavy heat of day by night.
В В В В Him can no God cast down, whom none
В В В В Can lift in hope beyond the height
В В В В Of fate and nature and things done
В В В В By the calm rule of might and right
В В В В That bids men be and bear and do,
В В В В And die beneath blind skies or blue.

В В В В To him the lights of even and morn
В В В В Speak no vain things of love or scorn,
В В В В Fancies and passions miscreate
В В В В By man in things dispassionate.
В В В В Nor holds he fellowship forlorn
В В В В With souls that pray and hope and hate,
В В В В And doubt they had better not been born,
В В В В And fain would lure or scare off fate
В В В В And charm their doomsman from their doom
В В В В And make fear dig its own false tomb.

В В В В He builds not half of doubts and half
В В В В Of dreams his own soulпїЅs cenotaph,
В В В В Whence hopes and fears with helpless eyes,
В В В В Wrapt loose in cast-off cerecloths, rise
В В В В And dance and wring their hands and laugh,
В В В В And weep thin tears and sigh light sighs,
В В В В And without living lips would quaff
В В В В The living spring in man that lies,
В В В В And drain his soul of faith and strength
В В В В It might have lived on a lifeпїЅs length.

В В В В He hath given himself and hath not sold
В В В В To God for heaven or man for gold,
В В В В Or grief for comfort that it gives,
В В В В Or joy for griefпїЅs restoratives.
В В В В He hath given himself to time, whose fold
В В В В Shuts in the mortal flock that lives
В В В В On its plain pastureпїЅs heat and cold
В В В В And the equal yearпїЅs alternatives.
В В В В Earth, heaven, and time, death, life, and he,
В В В В Endure while they shall be to be.

В В В В пїЅYet between death and life are hours
В В В В To flush with love and hide in flowers;
В В В В What profit save in these?пїЅ men cry:
В В В В пїЅAh, see, between soft earth and sky,
В В В В What only good things here are ours!пїЅ
В В В В They say, пїЅwhat better wouldst thou try,
В В В В What sweeter sing of? or what powers
В В В В Serve, that will give thee ere thou die
В В В В More joy to sing and be less sad,
В В В В More heart to play and grow more glad?пїЅ

В В В В Play then and sing; we too have played,
В В В В We likewise, in that subtle shade.
В В В В We too have twisted through our hair
В В В В Such tendrils as the wild Loves wear,
В В В В And heard what mirth the Maenads made,
В В В В Till the wind blew our garlands bare
В В В В And left their roses disarrayed,
В В В В And smote the summer with strange air,
В В В В And disengirdled and discrowned
В В В В The limbs and locks that vine-wreaths bound.

В В В В We too have tracked by star-proof trees
В В В В The tempest of the Thyiades
В В В В Scare the loud night on hills that hid
В В В В The blood-feasts of the Bassarid,
В В В В Heard their songпїЅs iron cadences
В В В В Fright the wolf hungering from the kid,
В В В В Outroar the lion-throated seas,
В В В В Outchide the north-wind if it chid,
В В В В And hush the torrent-tongued ravines
В В В В With thunders of their tambourines.

В В В В But the fierce flute whose notes acclaim
В В В В Dim goddesses of fiery fame,
В В В В Cymbal and clamorous kettledrum,
В В В В Timbrels and tabrets, all are dumb
В В В В That turned the high chill air to flame;
В В В В The singing tongues of fire are numb
В В В В That called on Cotys by her name1
В В В В Edonian, till they felt her come
В В В В And maddened, and her mystic face
В В В В Lightened along the streams of Thrace.

В В В В For Pleasure slumberless and pale,
В В В В And Passion with rejected veil,
В В В В Pass, and the tempest-footed throng
В В В В Of hours that follow them with song
В В В В Till their feet flag and voices fail,
В В В В And lips that were so loud so long
В В В В Learn silence, or a wearier wail;
В В В В So keen is change, and time so strong,
В В В В To weave the robes of life and rend
В В В В And weave again till life have end.

В В В В But weak is change, but strengthless time,
В В В В To take the light from heaven, or climb
В В В В The hills of heaven with wasting feet.
В В В В Songs they can stop that earth found meet,
В В В В But the stars keep their ageless rhyme;
В В В В Flowers they can slay that spring thought sweet,
В В В В But the stars keep their spring sublime;
В В В В Passions and pleasures can defeat,
В В В В Actions and agonies control,
В В В В And life and death, but not the soul.

В В В В Because manпїЅs soul is manпїЅs God still,
В В В В What wind soever waft his will
В В В В Across the waves of day and night
В В В В To port or shipwreck, left or right,
В В В В By shores and shoals of good and ill;
В В В В And still its flame at mainmast height
В В В В Through the rent air that foam-flakes fill
В В В В Sustains the indomitable light
В В В В Whence only man hath strength to steer
В В В В Or helm to handle without fear.

В В В В Save his own soulпїЅs light overhead,
В В В В None leads him, and none ever led,
В В В В Across birthпїЅs hidden harbour-bar,
В В В В Past youth where shoreward shallows are,
В В В В Through age that drives on toward the red
В В В В Vast void of sunset hailed from far,
В В В В To the equal waters of the dead;
В В В В Save his own soul he hath no star,
В В В В And sinks, except his own soul guide,
В В В В Helmless in middle turn of tide.

В В В В No blast of air or fire of sun
В В В В Puts out the light whereby we run
В В В В With girded loins our lamplit race,
В В В В And each from each takes heart of grace
В В В В And spirit till his turn be done,
В В В В And light of face from each manпїЅs face
В В В В In whom the light of trust is one;
В В В В Since only souls that keep their place
В В В В By their own light, and watch things roll,
В В В В And stand, have light for any soul.

В В В В A little time we gain from time
В В В В To set our seasons in some chime,
В В В В For harsh or sweet or loud or low,
В В В В With seasons played out long ago
В В В В And souls that in their time and prime
В В В В Took part with summer or with snow,
В В В В Lived abject lives out or sublime,
В В В В And had their chance of seed to sow
В В В В For service or disservice done
В В В В To those days dead and this their son.

В В В В A little time that we may fill
В В В В Or with such good works or such ill
В В В В As loose the bonds or make them strong
В В В В Wherein all manhood suffers wrong.
В В В В By rose-hung river and light-foot rill
В В В В There are who rest not; who think long
В В В В Till they discern as from a hill
В В В В At the sunпїЅs hour of morning song,
В В В В Known of souls only, and those souls free,
В В В В The sacred spaces of the sea.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Algernon Charles Swinburne delivers a powerful performance in "Prelude to Songs Before Sunrise"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
